subject: ap-government
title: Concept Application — California Governor Recall and Arnold Schwarzenegger
stimulus:
In 2003, California's Democratic governor Gray Davis' popularity was decreasing due to a budget shortfall and an energy crisis that received widespread coverage in the news. As a result, Republicans in the state initiated a recall election of the governor by gathering over one million signatures.

Recall is an electoral process by which citizens can vote to remove elected officials before the end of their term. There are provisions for recall elections of state officials in 19 states. In California, citizens initiate a recall election by collecting a minimum number of signatures on a petition, which puts the issue on a ballot in a subsequent election. If a majority of the voters choose to recall the elected official, then that official is removed from office. In California, the recall ballot asks voters two separate questions: Should the governor be recalled? And if so, who should be the new governor?

The front-running candidate to replace Governor Davis was action movie star Arnold Schwarzenegger, a Republican. Schwarzenegger announced his candidacy on a popular late-night television program, and his celebrity status drew national media attention. During his appearance, he said of Davis, "He's failing [Californians] terribly, and this is why he needs to be recalled." With the understanding that California had more registered Democrats than Republicans, Schwarzenegger strategically focused his campaign on his own personal story as a self-made immigrant and distanced himself from national Republican leaders.

In the final vote, Californians voted to recall Governor Davis, and Schwarzenegger was elected as his replacement. Exit polls showed that most voters who favored recalling Davis cited his recent performance as governor as their reason.

parts.prompt: Describe the model of voting behavior that best reflects most voters' choice to recall the governor in the scenario.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Describes the retrospective model of voting behavior. Accept: most voters used the retrospective model, which considers the elected official's past performance when deciding how to vote; voters took into account how Governor Davis performed in office when deciding whether to recall him.
parts.scoring_notes: Only the retrospective voting model earns the point. The response must explain what the model involves (evaluating past performance), not just name it.

parts.prompt: In the context of the scenario, explain how the media's role as a linkage institution might have affected the voting behavior described in part A.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Explains how media influenced the retrospective voting behavior. Accept: the media's reporting on Governor Davis's performance and his responsibility for the bad economy might have persuaded others to vote to recall Davis; media coverage brought attention to the energy crisis and budget shortfall, shaping voters' retrospective evaluation of Davis's record.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must connect media as a linkage institution to the specific retrospective voting behavior described in part A. Generic statements about media influence without connecting to the scenario do not earn the point.

parts.prompt: Explain how the electoral process for removing Governor Davis is different from the process for removing a sitting president of the United States.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Explains a specific difference between the recall process and presidential impeachment. Accept: in the scenario, a simple majority of voters is required to remove the governor, while a sitting U.S. president requires a two-thirds majority in the U.S. Senate; the California recall began when citizens collected signatures, while presidential removal begins when the House files formal charges approved by a majority vote; the recall is controlled by voters while presidential impeachment/removal is controlled by elected representatives.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must identify a specific procedural difference between the recall process and the federal presidential removal process. Accept any accurate comparison of at least one difference.
